steps.title: Identify what the data must show
steps.content: The assertion is that Drosophila collected in cooler months should have lower reproductive fitness. Wing centroid size is the proxy for body size, which correlates with fitness. To support the assertion, the data must show that a cooler month produced smaller wing centroid size than a warmer month — specifically for the same sex, so the comparison is clean.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• "the average male wing centroid size was 1.98 mm in September but was 2.31 mm in July.": The numeric values (1.98 mm in September, 2.31 mm in July) do not match the actual male wing centroid size data in the table. Because the question requires accurate table data, A misrepresents the data and cannot support any claim. ✗
  • "the average male wing centroid size was consistently smaller than the average female wing centroid size in all four months in the table.": This compares the two sexes within the same month — it says nothing about seasonal variation or cooler vs. warmer months. It cannot support a seasonal fitness claim. ✗
  • "the average monthly low temperature was lower in May than in September.": Temperature data alone, with no wing size measurement, cannot connect to reproductive fitness or body size. It omits the proxy measure entirely. ✗
  • "the average female wing centroid size was smaller in September than in July.": September is cooler than July; smaller wing centroid size means smaller body size, which means lower reproductive fitness. This directly illustrates the predicted relationship for female flies. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
